title: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(e) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.27: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.27' running on index=itsm (vuln disclosure tickets), index=email_gateway (parsed security@), HEC from bug bounty APIs, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:incident` (number, category, opened_at, work_start, state) ingested via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) using `/now/table/incident`; `index=email_gateway` `sourcetype=ms:o365:message` or `sourcetype=proofpoint:tap` for messages sent to `security@`; HEC JSON from HackerOne `reports` API (resource `/v1/reports`) landing in `index=bugbounty`.
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)), HTTP Event Collector global token for **bug bounty** and **secure mailbox** JSON.
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d
| eval opened=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triaged=strptime(work_start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triage_hours=round((triaged-opened)/3600,2)
| eval sla_breach=if(triage_hours>48 OR isnull(triaged),1,0)
| stats count as tickets sum(sla_breach) as breaches avg(triage_hours) as avg_triage
| eval breach_pct=round(100*breaches/tickets,1)
| table tickets, breaches, breach_pct, avg_triage
description: Aggregates ServiceNow incidents tagged for coordinated disclosure and calculates what share missed a 48-hour work-start SLA, producing breach rate and average triage hours for leadership review.
value: Researchers and regulators increasingly ask for proof you respond quickly; this search turns mailbox chaos into two numbers—percent late and average hours—suitable for an annual assurance letter without hand-counting tickets.
implementation: (1) Normalize `category` values in SNOW so disclosure incidents always match `*Vulnerability Disclosure*`. (2) Map `work_start` or `work_notes` first activity timestamp consistently — adjust `strptime` format to your locale. (3) Use HEC Ack for bug bounty ingestion at `/services/collector/event`. (4) Keep raw email in `index=email_gateway` with PII access restricted via RBAC. (5) Export monthly PDF to `index=audit_evidence`.
visualization: Single value breach_pct, line chart avg_triage, table of worst incidents with drilldown to SNOW.

references.title: ENISA Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure — Good practices

knownFalsePositives:
• **Major Sev-1 outage steals triage time** — Distinguish: incidents opened during documented crisis window with problem record. Suppress: `crisis_blackout.csv` time-bound exception register matched on opened_at.
• **Holidays or on-call rotation gaps** — Distinguish: no analyst logged `work_start` until Tuesday. Suppress: adjust SLA macro for bank holidays via lookup `holiday_calendar.csv`.
• **Duplicate SNOW records for same researcher email** — Distinguish: two incidents share external reporter hash. Suppress: `dedup number` after sort -_time or merge workflow in ServiceNow.
• **Bugcrowd API lag replays old reports** — Distinguish: `_time` cluster but `opened_at` stale. Suppress: filter HEC events `where payload_age_seconds < 900` at ingest time using **props.conf** `EVAL`.
• **Category typo breaks match** — Distinguish: team renamed category to `Vuln Disclosure`. Suppress: broaden search `where like(category,"%Disclosure%")` or maintain `nis2_vd_categories.csv` lookup.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements the vulnerability disclosure monitoring component of NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) and Art.12. NIS2 Art.12 specifically addresses coordinated vulnerability disclosure — Member States must have policies and procedures for coordinated disclosure, and entities must have mechanisms to receive, triage, and respond to vulnerability reports from external researchers, customers, or the public. Art.21(2)(e) further requires security in system maintenance, which includes vulnerability handling. This UC measures a concrete SLA: how quickly does the organisation triage (begin working on) a vulnerability disclosure report after it is received?
• The 48-hour SLA: the SPL uses a 48-hour threshold for initial triage (`triage_hours > 48`). This threshold is not prescribed by NIS2 itself but is aligned with ENISA's coordinated vulnerability disclosure good practice guidance, which recommends initial acknowledgement within 24-72 hours. The 48-hour threshold represents a pragmatic midpoint. For organisations with a published vulnerability disclosure policy (VDP), the SLA should match what is stated in the policy — typically 72 hours for acknowledgement and 48 hours for triage/work start. Configure the threshold as a macro for operational flexibility.
• ServiceNow incident integration: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) configured to pull incidents tagged with `category=Vulnerability Disclosure` (or your organisation's equivalent category). The TA's modular input queries `/api/now/table/incident` with filter `sysparm_query=categorySTARTSWITHVulnerability`. Key fields:
  - `number` — unique incident identifier (INC0012345)
  - `category` — must contain "Vulnerability Disclosure" (or match the wildcard in the SPL)
  - `opened_at` — timestamp when the incident was created (when the report was received)
  - `work_start` — timestamp when the assignee began working on the incident (triage start)
  - `state` — incident lifecycle state (New=1, In Progress=2, On Hold=3, Resolved=6, Closed=7)
  - `assigned_to` — the analyst assigned to triage the disclosure
  - `u_reporter_email` — custom field for the external researcher's contact (optional)
• The critical measurement is `triage_hours = (work_start - opened_at) / 3600`. This measures the time from incident creation to first work activity. `work_start` in ServiceNow is automatically populated when an analyst changes the state from "New" to "In Progress" or records the first work note. If `work_start` is null, it means the incident has not been triaged — this is treated as an SLA breach (`sla_breach=1` in the SPL's `isnull(triaged)` condition).
• The `work_start` field reliability: ServiceNow populates `work_start` based on analyst activity, but some teams use different workflow fields. Common alternatives: `u_first_response` (custom first-response timestamp), `sys_updated_on` (last modification timestamp — less precise but always populated), or the timestamp of the first `work_notes` entry. If `work_start` is consistently null in your environment, investigate which field your VD team actually uses and create a FIELDALIAS in `props.conf`.
• Bug bounty platform integration (optional but recommended): if your organisation uses HackerOne or Bugcrowd, configure HEC to ingest report data. HackerOne's API (`/v1/reports`) returns JSON with `attributes.created_at` (report submission time), `attributes.triaged_at` (triage timestamp), `attributes.state` (new, triaged, resolved, not-applicable, informative, duplicate). Configure a scripted input or Lambda function to poll the API and POST to HEC:
```ini
[http://hackerone_reports]
token = <HEC-TOKEN>
index = bugbounty
sourcetype = hackerone:report
```
• Security mailbox monitoring (optional): if your organisation's VDP directs researchers to email `security@example.com`, monitor this mailbox for disclosure volume. Use the Microsoft Office 365 TA (Splunkbase 4055) to pull messages from the shared mailbox, or Proofpoint TAP for email metadata. This provides a completeness check — are all email-reported disclosures being converted to ServiceNow incidents?
• Index retention: `index=itsm` ≥ 365 days, `index=bugbounty` ≥ 365 days, `index=email_gateway` ≥ 90 days, `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.
• RBAC: role `nis2_vd_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = itsm, bugbounty, email_gateway, audit_evidence`. Vulnerability disclosure data may contain details of unpatched security flaws — access must be restricted to the security response team.
• Exception handling: `crisis_blackout.csv` with columns `start_epoch`, `end_epoch`, `reason` (e.g., "Major outage — all-hands incident response"). During crisis periods, VD triage SLAs may legitimately be paused. The SPL can filter: `| lookup crisis_blackout.csv | where NOT (opened > start_epoch AND opened < end_epoch)`.
• Holiday calendar: `holiday_calendar.csv` with columns `date` (ISO 8601), `region` (if multi-region). The 48-hour SLA should count business hours only if your VDP states "within 2 business days." The current SPL uses wall-clock hours; for business-hour calculation, use `| eval business_hours = <custom calculation>` accounting for weekends and holidays.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**ServiceNow incident feed:**
Configure the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928). Navigate to Apps > ServiceNow TA > Configuration > Account. Provide:
- ServiceNow instance URL: `https://yourcompany.service-now.com`
- Authentication: OAuth 2.0 (recommended) or basic auth
- Enable the incident table input:
  - Table: `incident`
  - Filter: `categorySTARTSWITHVulnerability` (limits API volume to only VD incidents)
  - Interval: 300 seconds (5 minutes)
  - Index: `itsm`
  - Sourcetype: `snow:incident`

Verify the feed:
```
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d
| stats count dc(number) as incidents
| table count incidents
```
Expected: `incidents` should match the number of VD incidents in ServiceNow over the last 90 days. If `incidents=0`, check: (a) the `sysparm_query` filter in the TA input, (b) the `category` field name/value in your ServiceNow instance (some use `subcategory` or a custom field `u_category`), (c) TA logs for API errors.

**Field mapping verification:**
```
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d
| head 10
| table number, category, opened_at, work_start, state, assigned_to
```
Verify:
- `opened_at` contains a parseable timestamp. ServiceNow typically returns `2026-04-15 10:30:00` (no timezone — assumed UTC or your instance timezone).
- `work_start` is populated for incidents that have been triaged. If always null, investigate: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident | stats count(work_start) as has_ws count as total | eval pct=round(100*has_ws/total,1)`. If `pct=0`, the field is never populated — switch to an alternative field.
- `state` values: check `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident | stats count by state`. ServiceNow states are numeric (1=New, 2=In Progress, etc.) unless the TA translates them.

**Props.conf field alignment:**
If field names differ:
```ini
[snow:incident]
FIELDALIAS-work_start_alt = u_first_response AS work_start
FIELDALIAS-category_alt = subcategory AS category
```

**Bug bounty platform (HackerOne):**
If using HackerOne, configure a scripted input or scheduled Lambda to poll the API:
```
GET /v1/reports?filter[program][]=your_program&filter[state][]=new,triaged
```
The response JSON includes `data[].attributes.created_at` and `data[].attributes.triaged_at`. POST each report to HEC as a JSON event. Verify: `index=bugbounty sourcetype=hackerone:report earliest=-30d | stats count dc(id) as reports`.

**Security mailbox monitoring:**
If researchers email `security@example.com`, use the O365 TA to monitor the shared mailbox:
```ini
[o365:management:activity]
index = email_gateway
sourcetype = ms:o365:message
```
Verify: `index=email_gateway sourcetype=ms:o365:message recipient="security@example.com" earliest=-30d | stats count`. Cross-check with ServiceNow: are all emails being converted to incidents?

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.27 NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure SLA`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")` and `strptime(work_start, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")`**: converts ServiceNow timestamp strings to epoch for arithmetic. The format string must match exactly. ServiceNow default format is `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` without timezone. If your instance returns ISO 8601 with timezone (`2026-04-15T10:30:00+00:00`), use `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%:z`.

• **`triage_hours = round((triaged - opened) / 3600, 2)`**: calculates wall-clock hours between incident creation and first work activity. The division by 3600 converts seconds to hours. Rounding to 2 decimal places gives precision like "23.45 hours."

• **`sla_breach = if(triage_hours > 48 OR isnull(triaged), 1, 0)`**: two breach conditions:
  1. `triage_hours > 48` — the incident was triaged but took longer than 48 hours.
  2. `isnull(triaged)` — the incident has not been triaged at all (`work_start` is null). This catches incidents that are sitting in the queue untouched — the worst case from a compliance perspective.

• **Aggregation (`stats count as tickets sum(sla_breach) as breaches avg(triage_hours) as avg_triage`)**: produces three headline metrics:
  - `tickets` — total VD incidents in the period (volume indicator)
  - `breaches` — number of incidents that missed the SLA (failure count)
  - `avg_triage` — average triage time in hours (efficiency indicator)
  The `breach_pct = round(100 * breaches / tickets, 1)` gives the SLA compliance rate as a percentage.

Schedule: cron `0 7 1 * *` (monthly, 1st of the month at 07:00). Time range: `earliest=-90d latest=now`. Trigger: `breach_pct > 15` (alert if more than 15% of disclosures breach the SLA) OR `avg_triage > 36` (alert if average triage exceeds 36 hours — warning threshold before the 48-hour breach).

Alert actions: (1) Email to the Head of Incident Response with `breach_pct` and `avg_triage`. (2) If `breach_pct > 25`: PagerDuty page to the CISO — more than a quarter of disclosures are breaching SLA. (3) Write summary to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:vd:sla`.

Extended search — per-incident detail:
For root cause analysis when the SLA is breached, run the non-aggregated version:
```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d
| eval opened=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triaged=strptime(work_start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triage_hours=round((triaged-opened)/3600,2)
| eval sla_breach=if(triage_hours>48 OR isnull(triaged),1,0)
| where sla_breach=1
| table number, category, opened_at, work_start, triage_hours, assigned_to, state
| sort triage_hours desc
```
This shows individual breaching incidents with their assigned analysts — useful for identifying systemic issues (one analyst consistently slow, weekend incidents always breaching, etc.).

Extended search — HackerOne correlation:
```spl
| append [
    search index=bugbounty sourcetype=hackerone:report earliest=-90d
    | eval h1_opened=strptime('attributes.created_at',"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ")
    | eval h1_triaged=strptime('attributes.triaged_at',"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ")
    | eval h1_triage_hours=round((h1_triaged-h1_opened)/3600,2)
    | eval sla_breach=if(h1_triage_hours>48 OR isnull(h1_triaged),1,0)
    | stats count as tickets sum(sla_breach) as breaches avg(h1_triage_hours) as avg_triage
    | eval source="HackerOne"
]
```
This appends bug bounty platform metrics alongside ServiceNow metrics, providing a unified view of all disclosure channels.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Timestamp accuracy**: select five incidents from the results. In ServiceNow, navigate to each incident and verify `Opened` and `Work Started` timestamps match `opened_at` and `work_start` in Splunk. Calculate `triage_hours` manually: `(work_start_epoch - opened_epoch) / 3600`. Acceptable deviation: ±0.1 hours (timezone or rounding differences).

(b) **SLA breach confirmation**: select three incidents flagged as `sla_breach=1`. For each, verify in ServiceNow that `work_start` is either: (a) more than 48 hours after `opened_at`, or (b) null (no work started). If the ServiceNow UI shows that work was done within 48 hours but `work_start` is null in Splunk, the field mapping is wrong — investigate which field ServiceNow actually populates.

(c) **Count parity**: run `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d | stats count` and compare with ServiceNow's list view (Incident > All > Filter: Category = Vulnerability Disclosure, Created > 90 days ago). They should match within ±2 (timing differences from the TA's polling interval).

(d) **`strptime` null test**: run `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" | eval test=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | stats count(eval(isnull(test))) as null_timestamps`. If `null_timestamps > 0`, the timestamp format doesn't match — inspect `opened_at` values and adjust the format string.

(e) **Negative test**: if all VD incidents in the last 90 days were triaged within 48 hours, the search should return `breach_pct=0` and `breaches=0`. Verify the metrics show a clean state. If not, investigate the breaching incident.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure SLA`):
• **Row 1 — KPI tiles**: Single-value `SLA breach rate %` (green ≤ 5%, amber ≤ 15%, red > 15%). Single-value `Average triage hours` (green ≤ 24h, amber ≤ 48h, red > 48h). Single-value `Total disclosures (90d)` (volume). Single-value `Untriaged disclosures` (count of incidents with null `work_start` — red ≥ 1).
• **Row 2 — Triage time histogram**: histogram of `triage_hours` showing the distribution. A healthy distribution clusters below 24 hours with a long tail. If bimodal (peaks at 4h and 72h), the team responds quickly during business hours but slowly during weekends.
• **Row 3 — Timeline**: `timechart span=7d avg(triage_hours) as avg_triage count as volume` over the last 6 months. Shows whether triage time is improving or degrading over time.
• **Row 4 — Worst incidents table**: sortable table of the 10 slowest-triaged incidents with `number`, `opened_at`, `work_start`, `triage_hours`, `assigned_to`. Drilldown to `https://<instance>.service-now.com/nav_to.do?uri=incident.do?sysparm_query=number=<number>`.
• **Row 5 — Mailbox correlation** (optional): compare `index=email_gateway recipient="security@*" | timechart count as emails` with `index=itsm category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" | timechart count as incidents`. If emails significantly exceed incidents, some disclosures are not being converted to tickets — a process gap.

Runbook (owner: Head of Incident Response / VD Coordinator):
1. Monthly: review the SLA report. For each breaching incident:
   - Identify root cause: was the analyst on leave? Was the incident mis-categorised and routed to the wrong team? Was the security mailbox unmonitored during a holiday?
   - Document the root cause and corrective action in the incident's work notes.
   - If systemic (e.g., all weekend disclosures breach): adjust the on-call rotation to include VD triage coverage.
2. For untriaged disclosures (`work_start` null): these are the highest priority. Contact the assigned analyst. If no analyst is assigned, escalate to the VD coordinator for immediate triage and assignment.
3. Quarterly: generate the VD SLA report for the CISO and legal team. This report is evidence for NIS2 Art.12 compliance. Include: `breach_pct`, `avg_triage`, trend over 4 quarters, and corrective actions taken for breaches. Sign with RFC3161 and archive.
4. Annually: review the 48-hour SLA threshold against the published VDP. If the VDP states "within 3 business days" but the SPL measures wall-clock hours, adjust either the VDP or the search to ensure alignment.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`strptime` returns null — all `triage_hours` are null.** The `opened_at` or `work_start` timestamp format doesn't match `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`. Common variations: (a) ISO 8601 with T separator: `2026-04-15T10:30:00` — fix: `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S`. (b) With timezone: `2026-04-15 10:30:00 UTC` — fix: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z`. (c) European format: `15/04/2026 10:30:00` — fix: `%d/%m/%Y %H:%M:%S`. Test with: `| makeresults | eval t="2026-04-15T10:30:00" | eval e=strptime(t,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") | table e`.

• **`work_start` always null — all incidents show `sla_breach=1`.** The ServiceNow instance doesn't populate `work_start` for your VD workflow. Common alternatives: (a) `u_first_response` — a custom field for first analyst response. (b) `sys_updated_on` — last modification timestamp (less precise but always populated). (c) The timestamp of the first `work_notes` entry — requires a separate query to `sys_journal_field`. Fix: create a FIELDALIAS in `props.conf` to map the correct field to `work_start`.

• **Zero incidents — `category` filter matches nothing.** The `category` value in ServiceNow doesn't contain "Vulnerability Disclosure". Check: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident | stats count by category | sort - count | head 20`. If the VD category is named differently (e.g., "Security Vulnerability", "CVD Report", "Bug Bounty"), update the SPL's `category` filter. For resilience, use a lookup `nis2_vd_categories.csv` with all valid category names and join instead of hardcoding.

• **Duplicate incidents inflating `tickets` count.** ServiceNow incidents can be reopened, which creates new events in Splunk for the same `number`. The `stats` command aggregates all events, potentially counting the same incident multiple times. Fix: deduplicate before aggregation: `| dedup number sortby - _time` keeps only the most recent event per incident number.

• **HEC 400 errors from bug bounty platform.** The JSON payload from HackerOne or Bugcrowd is not wrapped in the HEC event format. Fix: the payload must be `{"event": {...}}`. If the upstream system sends raw JSON, configure a middleware (Lambda, Cloud Function) to wrap it. Alternatively, use HEC's raw endpoint (`/services/collector/raw`) with `sourcetype` in the URL query string.

• **SLA measurement doesn't account for business hours.** The 48-hour threshold measures wall-clock time, not business hours. A disclosure received Friday at 17:00 and triaged Monday at 09:00 shows `triage_hours=64` (breach) even though only 8 business hours elapsed. Fix: implement a business-hour calculation using `holiday_calendar.csv` and a custom eval: count only hours within 09:00-17:00 on non-holiday weekdays. This is complex in SPL; consider a custom search command or Python-based calculation.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how quickly real people answer when someone emails a security weakness, so you can show the world you treat those messages seriously instead of letting them gather dust.
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.27` monthly workbook, ServiceNow incident export hash, and HEC receipt IDs archived under the vulnerability disclosure control folder.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Import fixture `snow:incident` rows where half the tickets never receive `work_start` within 48 hours of `opened_at`; the summary panel shows `breach_pct` above zero and `avg_triage` above the policy line.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Replay fixtures where every incident records `work_start` within six hours and categories strictly follow the disclosure naming standard; the search shows `breach_pct` equal to zero while ticket volume remains non-zero.

equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, incident table ingestion for VD tickets)
HackerOne (bug bounty platform, report API via HEC)
Bugcrowd (optional alternative bug bounty platform, report API via HEC)
Microsoft Office 365 TA (Splunkbase 4055, security@ mailbox monitoring)
